stdout:
Now I have enough context and precedent to produce the marketing output for the coin-locker research.

---

# Marketing — Kabukicho Survival Map

**Business:** kabukicho_survival_map | **Role:** marketing | **Task:** auto-0008, built on market_research/task-0007-locker-deep

Per contract, this is read-only marketing output for the execution adapter to persist as the artifact — no files modified.

## Building on prior work

- auto-0001: core positioning — confirmed / caution / gray-zone orientation guide.
- task-0002/auto-0002/task-0001-promo-plan: essentials copy (lockers, ATMs, toilets, lodging); flagged "coin lockers reported full by 10am" as a standout hook back when the dataset had only 1 generic locker entry.
- auto-0004/auto-0005/auto-0006/auto-0007: established the recurring correction pattern — each deep-dive found the old dataset undercounting a category by roughly an order of magnitude (2→18 conbinis, 1→14 ATMs), plus a category-specific "trap" (customer-only restrooms, Japan Post ATMs closing at 5pm).
- **task-0007-locker-deep (this task's input):** 16 named, addressed coin-locker/luggage-storage entries, up from the old dataset's single generic "JR Shinjuku Station lockers" pin — continuing the same undercount-correction pattern, now for lockers. Two structural findings distinguish this dataset from the ATM/conbini ones: (1) Zepp Shinjuku's 840 lockers are entry-gated — usable only *after* buying a ticket and passing the gate, not for pre-show luggage storage; (2) Kabukicho has a genuine cash-only/cashless-only split (Seibu-Shinjuku North Exit and Tokyu Tower's 4F/10F lockers are cashless-only via IC card or app; Zepp, Humax Pavilion, Godzilla Road, and Tokyu B1F are cash-only).

## Positioning for this dataset

**The lockers you'll actually find open, sized right, and payable with what's in your pocket — not just "there are lockers at the station."** The old assumption ("Shinjuku Station has lockers, done") breaks down three separate ways in this research: station lockers are commonly gone by 10am on a normal day; the one venue with the most lockers in Kabukicho itself (Zepp Shinjuku, 840 units) won't let you use them unless you're already past the ticket gate; and roughly half the district's lockers won't take the payment method you're carrying. This product's edge is naming which specific locker matches your bag, your cash situation, and your timing — not just confirming that lockers exist somewhere nearby.

## Audience segments to target

1. **Same-day arrivals with luggage, deciding where to stash before exploring** — likely to default to "the station" without knowing large lockers are frequently gone by mid-morning, or that Kabukicho itself has closer options.
2. **Concertgoers heading to Zepp Shinjuku who assume they can pre-stash a bag before the show** — a distinct failure mode nobody else's guide flags: Zepp's lockers are venue-customer-only, not walk-up.
3. **Travelers whose suitcase won't fit any standard coin locker** — a gap physical lockers structurally can't solve; ecbo cloak's staffed, no-size-cap storage is the actual answer, not another locker recommendation.

## Channel-specific copy

**1. Audience: Same-day arrivals with luggage, defaulting to "just use the station lockers."**
**Channel: Travel forum / subreddit post (r/JapanTravel, r/tokyo) — practical Q&A style.**

> Shinjuku Station's lockers (3,600+ across all exits) are the biggest bank around, but the large ones are commonly gone by 10am — if you're arriving mid-morning with a suitcase, don't count on one being free. Two closer alternatives sit inside Kabukicho itself: a 24h, 300+ unit bank at B1F of Tokyu Kabukicho Tower near the Ichibangai arch (cash-only, ~1 min from the entrance), and a smaller locker at the entrance of Karaoke-kan's Kabukicho Main Store on Godzilla Road. Note the Tokyu B1F bank isn't listed on the tower's own official FAQ — it only advertises a staffed 1F baggage desk — so it's independently documented by visitor guides rather than the venue itself; worth a quick look before you commit if you're carrying something valuable.

**2. Audience: Concertgoers heading to Zepp Shinjuku, assuming pre-show luggage storage is available.**
**Channel: Music/concert-going Facebook groups and Discord communities for Tokyo venue-goers.**

> Don't show up to Zepp Shinjuku planning to dump your bag in their lockers before the doors open — all 840 of them (690 at B1F, 150 at B4F) sit *past* the ticket gate, so you can only use them once you've already scanned in. If you need to stash luggage before the show, go to the Tokyu Kabukicho Tower B1F locker bank instead (same building, same basement level, but on the public side of the gate) or the Karaoke-kan entrance locker on Godzilla Road nearby. Bring cash either way — Zepp's own lockers run ¥400 cash-only if you do end up using them post-entry.

**3. Audience: Travelers whose suitcase doesn't fit any standard coin locker.**
**Channel: Short-form video script outline / X thread.**

> Hook: "Your suitcase doesn't fit in a Japanese coin locker. Here's what actually works." Beat 1: quick visual of standard locker size tiers (S/M/L) across Kabukicho and Shinjuku Station, showing where the size ceiling tops out even at the "large" tier. Beat 2 (the payoff, delivered here, not teased): ecbo cloak has a confirmed partner location inside Kabukicho itself (H.F. Kabukicho Building, 1-2-2) — app-reserved, staffed, coin-locker-priced, and with no rigid size cap, meaning it's the actual fix when your bag is oversized, not just "try a bigger locker." Beat 3: the tradeoff — it's app-reservation-based and staffed-hours, not a 24h walk-up locker, so it suits a planned drop-off, not a spontaneous 3am stash. CTA: "Full locker + luggage-storage map linked" (link pending deployment).
> *(Provisional: requires the live product to demo on-screen — cannot be scripted further until deployment exists, per the standing deployment caveat from task-0001-promo-plan.)*

## Notes on scope

- The three Tokyu Kabukicho Tower locker entries (B1F/4F/10F) and the Hotel WAKO/Manboo Manboo entries carry `reliability_score: 2-3` in the research — sourced from independent blogs, not the venues' own sites. Copy above names only the B1F bank specifically (and flags its unofficial sourcing directly in copy #1) rather than treating any of the three as equally solid; the 4F/10F entries and Hotel WAKO/Manboo Manboo are not named in any copy piece.
- The Zepp Shinjuku entry-gate restriction is carried as stated in the research (`access_restriction: venue_customers_only`, `reliability_score: 4`) — treated as solid enough to state plainly rather than hedge, consistent with its sourcing from two independent blogs plus the structural logic of ticketed-venue lockers.
- No copy claims a bag will definitely fit a given locker size tier — sizing is described qualitatively (S/M/L, "doesn't fit"), not with exact dimensions, since none were in the source research.
- No copy has been posted or scheduled. Per contract, this role has no posting IO; publishing requires the separate policy-gated pipeline (ADR-0035) and human finalization.

## Self-critique (changes made before finalizing)

- **Specificity**: an earlier draft of copy #1 said the station lockers "fill up fast" — revised to the actual sourced detail (large lockers commonly gone by 10am) and named two specific alternative locations instead of a generic "try elsewhere" suggestion.
- **Payoff completeness**: copy #3's hook ("here's what actually works") is resolved in the same piece by naming the actual location (ecbo cloak, H.F. Kabukicho Building) rather than left as a stub; Beat 3 also delivers the honest tradeoff (staffed hours, not 24h) instead of stopping at the win.
- **Experience honesty**: no first-person "I stashed my bag there" claims — all copy uses "confirmed," "documented," and explicitly flags which specific claim (Tokyu B1F) is sourced from independent guides rather than the venue's own site, matching the research's own reliability scoring.
- **Template-detection**: the three pieces vary structure on purpose — station-default-correction / venue-specific-trap-warning / hook-then-solution-then-honest-tradeoff — continuing the varied-structure pattern from auto-0005/auto-0006/auto-0007 rather than reusing one template. Copy #2 in particular is a new structure for this series (venue-specific warning aimed at a narrower audience than prior pieces), since Zepp's gated-access finding doesn't fit the "correction to a common assumption" frame the other two pieces use.

Marketing content produced and returned to the execution adapter — no files written by this task.
